hints.junior: The console snippet is your fastest tool: paste it in DevTools and it logs every element whose right edge goes past the viewport.
hints.mid: Don't add overflow-x: hidden to html or body: it silently breaks position: sticky on every element inside. Fix the source element instead.
hints.staff: Horizontal overflow in production is often introduced by a new image or SVG without max-width: 100%. Add this to your CI visual regression checks.

hints.junior: See how one wide element creates a horizontal scrollbar for the whole page? That's why finding the culprit is more important than hiding the overflow.
hints.mid: min-width: 0 is the fix for flex children that overflow. The default min-width: auto prevents flex items from shrinking below their content width.
hints.staff: The fixed demo uses both max-width: 100% and min-width: 0; these two properties address the two most common overflow mechanisms.

hints.junior: Five usual suspects cause horizontal overflow: a fixed width wider than the viewport, an unbreakable long string, a negative margin, an absolutely-positioned element, or an SVG missing its viewBox. Check those first before guessing.
hints.mid: Paste the debugger snippet in DevTools console and it logs every element whose right edge crosses the viewport, sorted by how far it overflows — faster than eyeballing the DOM tree.
hints.staff: The audit-mode trick — a one-line CSS rule that outlines every element on the page — turns 20 minutes of DOM inspection into a 5-second visual scan for the culprit.

hints.junior: Two fixes cover most horizontal-overflow bugs: max-width: 100% on images and SVGs, and min-width: 0 on flex children. Try both before digging deeper.
hints.mid: 100vw includes the scrollbar gutter on platforms with persistent scrollbars, so it renders 15 to 17px wider than 100% on desktop Chrome and Firefox — and on iOS Safari it still overflows the same way despite the scrollbar being an overlay.
hints.staff: overflow-x: clip does the same job as hidden without establishing a new block formatting context, meaning it won't silently break position: sticky the way hidden does — the safer default for clipping decorative overflow.

anecdotes.junior: I once spent 30 minutes hunting a horizontal scrollbar on mobile. It was a 600px-wide SVG in a marketing section that had no viewBox. The console snippet found it in 3 seconds.
anecdotes.mid: overflow-x: hidden on body is the most copied StackOverflow 'fix', and it breaks sticky headers on the same page. The console snippet is always the right first step.
anecdotes.staff: Min-width: 0 on flex children is the most commonly missing CSS in component library audits. It's a spec quirk that surprises even experienced CSS authors.

sections.junior: Hey! Today we're hunting down that annoying horizontal scrollbar that shows up on mobile. One JS snippet will find the culprit in seconds.
sections.mid: This case covers horizontal overflow: the console debugging snippet, why overflow-x: hidden on body breaks sticky, and the CSS defaults that prevent this.
sections.staff: We're covering the full overflow debugging toolkit: getBoundingClientRect audit, min-width: 0 for flex, and CI integration to catch this before it ships.

sections.junior: The concept chapter is the principle. Read it before the demo so the toggle makes sense.
sections.mid: Connect the principle to your component API: what prop or CSS change encodes the fix?
sections.staff: Map the pattern to observability: what would you measure in RUM or lab to prove the fix?

sections.junior: See how one wide element creates a horizontal scrollbar for the whole page? That's why finding the culprit is more important than hiding the overflow.
sections.mid: min-width: 0 is the fix for flex children that overflow. The default min-width: auto prevents flex items from shrinking below their content width.
sections.staff: The fixed demo uses both max-width: 100% and min-width: 0; these two properties address the two most common overflow mechanisms.
